---
title: "Week 4: Design of Experiments"
subtitle: "STAT9004 — Statistical Methods"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: true
    theme: cosmo
    code-fold: true
    code-summary: "Show R Code"
---

```{=html}
<!-- ============================================================
     STUDY GUIDE — Week 4: Design of Experiments
     Sources: Lecture slides, class transcript (Paula Harrison),
              Worksheet 2 R script (WS2_sample_size_script_STAT9004.R)
============================================================ -->
```

---

::: {.callout-warning}
## ⚠️ Assessment Reminders

- **No class next week** — the professor confirmed this at the start of the session.
- **Worksheet 2** (Sample Size, Effect Size and Significance) was covered in this session — review the R script and the exercises before the next class.
- **Exam-style questions on experimental design terms** (factors, levels, treatments, experimental units) were explicitly flagged by the professor as likely exam content.
- Power analysis questions — identifying Type I and Type II errors in context, calculating power and required sample size — were practiced in class and in the worksheet.
:::

---

# Key Topics

This week covered two connected areas:

- **Fundamentals of Experimental Design** — how to plan a well-structured study
- **Association vs. Causation** — a critical concept in all data analysis
- **Extraneous, Confounding, and Spurious Variables** — what can go wrong in a study
- **Key Experimental Design Terms** — factors, levels, treatments, experimental units
- **Randomisation, Blocking, and Replication** — the three principles of good experimental design
- **ANOVA (Introduction/Preview)** — extension of the t-test to three or more groups
- **Effect Size, Sample Size, and Statistical Power** — how these three interact
- **Power Analysis in R** — using the `pwr` package

---

# Core Explanations

## What is Experimental Design?

**Experimental design** is the process of planning a study *before* you collect any data. The goal is to make sure you collect the right data, in the right amount, using the right methods — so that your conclusions are valid and objective.

Think of it like preparing a recipe before you cook. If you don't plan what ingredients you need and in what order to add them, the result may not be what you expected.

Before running any study, you need to decide:

1. A clear **research question** — one that can be turned into testable hypotheses (H₀ and Hₐ).
2. An **analysis plan** — which variables will you measure? Are they categorical or numeric? Which statistical test is appropriate?
3. A **significance level** (usually α = 0.05) — decided *before* looking at the data.
4. How to avoid **p-hacking** — do not keep changing your analysis until you get a significant result.

::: {.callout-important}
## ⭐ Professor's Emphasis: p-Hacking

*Professor's note:* "If you don't get the result that you want, you don't modify your analysis to get what you want, or you don't modify or look at a different variable so that you get a statistically significant result. A null result is also important and should be encouraged to publish."

**p-hacking** (also called *data dredging*) means looking at many variables or analyses until you find a p-value below 0.05. This is dishonest and scientifically invalid. A null result — finding no significant difference — is still a valid result.
:::

---

### The Three Principles of Efficient Experimental Design

*Professor's note:* "There are three basic principles to increase efficiency, and they are randomization… blocking… and replication."

| Principle | What it means | Why it matters |
|---|---|---|
| **Randomisation** | Every experimental unit has an equal chance of being in any treatment group | Eliminates selection bias; satisfies the assumption that observations are independent |
| **Blocking** | Group similar units together first, then randomise within each group | Similar to stratified random sampling — reduces the effect of known sources of variation and adds precision |
| **Replication** | Repeat each experimental condition independently | Lets you estimate experimental error; more replication = more precise estimates and greater power to detect real differences |

::: {.callout-note}
## 💡 Analogy — Blocking

Imagine you want to test a new fertiliser on crops. If one side of the field gets more sun than the other, the sunnier side might grow better *regardless* of the fertiliser. By **blocking** (dividing the field into sun/shade blocks first, then applying treatments within each block), you separate the effect of sunlight from the effect of the fertiliser.
:::

---

## Experimental Studies vs. Observational Studies

It is essential to distinguish between the two types of study when interpreting results.

| Feature | Experimental Study | Observational Study |
|---|---|---|
| Researcher role | **Intervenes / manipulates** conditions | **Observes** existing conditions only |
| Randomisation | ✅ Possible | ❌ Not possible |
| Confounding | Can be controlled via **design** | Must be controlled **statistically** |
| Causal inference | Stronger — can claim causation | Weaker — can only claim association |
| Example | Clinical trial (drug vs placebo) | Survey on coffee and heart disease |
| Ethical issues | Sometimes not possible to manipulate | Often the only ethical option |

*Professor's note:* "In an experiment, the researcher intervenes or manipulates the environmental conditions. So they've set up the conditions under which the experiment is going to be run. Whereas, with an observational study, the researcher is just observing the existing situation. Some studies are primarily observational studies — down to ethical reasons."

::: {.callout-note}
## 💡 Quick Rule for Identifying Study Type

Ask yourself: *Did the researcher set the conditions under which the data were collected?*

- **Yes** → Experimental study
- **No** → Observational study
:::

### Two Main Problems in Observational Studies

1. **Non-random selection** — If participants are not chosen randomly, the sample may not represent the general population.
2. **Uncontrolled extraneous variables** — Variables you did not account for that may affect the outcome.

---

## Association vs. Causation

::: {.callout-important}
## ⭐ Core Concept — "Association Is Not Causation"

*Professor's note:* "Everybody kind of knows that association is not causation, but I don't think everybody actually understands what it means."

**Association** means two variables move together in a systematic way — as one increases, the other tends to increase (or decrease). This does **not** mean one *causes* the other.
:::

### Types of Association

| Type | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Statistical association** | Variables are correlated; no causation implied | X and Y move together statistically |
| **Causal association** | One variable **directly** causes a change in the other | Sugar consumption → tooth decay |
| **Non-causal association** | A **third variable** explains the relationship | Coffee and heart disease — both linked to smoking |
| **Chance / Spurious association** | The relationship is a coincidence | Ice cream sales and shark attacks — both linked to hot weather |

*Professor's note (on sugar and tooth decay):* "Sugar consumption would not 100% cause tooth decay. It's direct, but it's not on its own. So we have other factors to consider — oral bacteria, saliva, fluoride, diet, oral hygiene."

### Common Measures of Association

| Measure | Type | When to use |
|---|---|---|
| **Pearson's r (or ρ)** | Parametric | Linear association between two numeric variables |
| **Spearman's Rank ρ** | Non-parametric | When data is ordered/ranked, or non-linear association; tests median |
| **Chi-square (χ²)** | Categorical | Association between two categorical variables |
| **Odds Ratio / Relative Risk** | Categorical | Used in logistic regression and epidemiology |

*Professor's note:* "Pearson's correlation coefficient — r represents the sample, and rho represents the population. That's a parametric association. Spearman's rank is non-parametric — the test statistic you're studying is the median."

---

## Extraneous, Confounding, and Spurious Variables

Understanding these three concepts is essential for interpreting any study correctly.

### Extraneous Variables

**Extraneous variables** are any variables *other than the independent variable* that may affect the result. They are not the main variable of interest.

They can be:

- **Controlled** — the researcher accounts for them in the study design.
- **Uncontrolled** — they are not accounted for, and this can cause problems.

### Confounding Variables

A **confounding variable** is a *specific type* of uncontrolled extraneous variable that:

- Influences **both** the independent variable **and** the dependent variable
- Makes it impossible to determine whether the observed effect is truly caused by the independent variable, or by the confounding variable

*Professor's note:* "Just think of a confounding variable as being a third variable that you cannot see. This third variable will influence both the independent and the dependent variable, and the relationship between these two variables that you're studying can be either fully or only partially explained by the third variable."

::: {.callout-warning}
## ⚠️ Common Mistake — Confounding vs. Extraneous

All **confounding** variables are extraneous variables.
But **not all** extraneous variables are confounding.

An extraneous variable only becomes confounding if it influences *both* the independent variable *and* the dependent variable at the same time.
:::

::: {.callout-note}
## 💡 Example: Alcohol, Age, and HDL Cholesterol

A study looks at the effect of alcohol consumption on HDL (good) cholesterol levels in a group of males.

- **Independent variable:** Alcohol consumption
- **Dependent variable:** HDL cholesterol
- **Confounding variable:** Age

Why is age confounding? Because age independently affects cholesterol levels — as people get older, cholesterol changes naturally. So any association observed between alcohol and cholesterol *could be partly or entirely due to age*, not alcohol itself.

In a linear model, age would be *included and held constant* to control for this:

$$\text{HDL} = \alpha + \beta_1 \cdot \text{Alcohol} + \beta_2 \cdot \text{Age} + \varepsilon$$

*Professor's note:* "We're only interested in the effect of alcohol on HDL levels. Age is included there — it's held constant, but we're controlling for that confounding variable."
:::

### Controlling for Confounding

| Study Type | How confounding is controlled |
|---|---|
| **Experimental** | **Randomisation** — the gold standard. Participants are randomly assigned, which distributes confounders evenly across groups |
| **Observational** | **Statistical methods** — linear regression, logistic regression — hold confounders constant mathematically |

*Professor's note:* "Randomisation is the key. Randomized controlled trials are considered the gold standard in research for evaluating the effectiveness or the safety of a new treatment."

### Spurious Correlation

A **spurious correlation** is when two variables appear to be associated, but there is no logical or causal reason for the association — it is a coincidence.

*Professor's note:* "A spurious correlation is when one variable is associated with another but there's no plausible or logical reason for the association. The relationship is perceived as real but is coincidence. The example that comes up for spurious correlation is ice cream sales and drownings. They appear associated — but it's actually spurious. They're not related. What's the real driver? Weather — summer causes both."

---

## Key Terms in Experimental Design

::: {.callout-important}
## ⭐ Exam Tip — Know These Terms in Context

*Professor's note:* "The type of exam question that might appear is that you could be given a scenario of an experiment, and then from there you have to identify: What are the experimental units? What are the treatments? What's the response variable? How many treatment groups are there? How many factors? How many levels? What is the design?"
:::

| Term | Definition | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Experimental unit** | The subject or item on which the experiment is performed | Patients, animals, soil samples |
| **Factor** | A *controlled* explanatory variable whose levels are set by the experimenter | Type of drug; Vitamin C (yes/no) |
| **Level** | Each distinct setting or category of a factor | Drug A, Drug B, Drug C = 3 levels |
| **Treatment** | A specific combination of factor levels applied to one experimental unit | Drug A + Vitamin C = Treatment 1 |
| **Response variable** | The outcome being measured (= dependent variable, Y) | HDL cholesterol level |
| **Explanatory variable** | The variable used to explain the outcome (= independent variable, X) | Alcohol consumption |

::: {.callout-note}
## 💡 Tip — How to Identify Independent vs. Dependent Variable

*Professor's note:* "If you don't know which is the independent and which is the dependent — put it into a sentence. Does tooth decay depend on sugar consumption? Or is sugar consumption dependent on tooth decay? That generally almost clears it up."
:::

### Worked Exam Example: Drug + Vitamin C Study

*Six groups of patients are each treated with one of three different drugs, either in combination with Vitamin C or alone.*

| | Drug A | Drug B | Drug C |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Vitamin C** | Treatment 1 | Treatment 2 | Treatment 3 |
| **No Vitamin C** | Treatment 4 | Treatment 5 | Treatment 6 |

| Question | Answer |
|---|---|
| Experimental units | Patients |
| Number of factors | **2** (Type of Drug; Vitamin C) |
| Levels per factor | Drug: **3** levels (A, B, C); Vitamin C: **2** levels (yes/no) |
| Number of treatment groups | **6** (= 2 × 3) |
| Design | **Factorial design** |
| Statistical test | **Two-way ANOVA** (two factors) |

*Professor's note:* "Think of this as a 2 by 3 table — like a contingency table. The headers are the factors. The cells are the treatment combinations."

---

## Randomised Designs and Blind Studies

### Completely Randomised Design (CRD)

All experimental units are randomly assigned to treatment groups. Every unit has an equal chance of receiving any treatment.

- Eliminates **selection bias**
- Satisfies the statistical assumption that observations are **independent**
- Randomisation also averages out the effects of extraneous variables

*Professor's note:* "In a completely randomised design, all possible allocations are equally likely — every member of the population has the same equally likely chance of being selected as every other member."

### Randomised Block Design (RBD)

When known differences between units exist (e.g., gender, age group), units are first divided into **homogeneous blocks**, then randomly assigned to treatment groups *within* each block.

*Example:* In the osteoporosis drug study, giving the new drug to all men and the placebo to all women is **wrong** — it does not control for gender differences. The correct approach is to split patients into male and female blocks first, then randomly assign drug/placebo within each block.

| Block Design Type | Description |
|---|---|
| **Complete block** | Every block contains every treatment |
| **Incomplete block** | Not all treatments appear in every block (adjusted statistically) |

### Blind Studies — Reducing Bias Further

| Design | Who is unaware of treatment assignment |
|---|---|
| **Single blind** | Participants only |
| **Double blind** | Participants **and** the researchers administering the treatment |
| **Triple blind** | Participants, researchers, **and** the data analysts |

*Professor's note:* "A single blind experiment is when the participants don't know what treatment group they belong to. That's generally the case — otherwise you're just introducing bias."

::: {.callout-note}
## 💡 Why Blind Studies Matter

If a patient *knows* they received the real drug (not a placebo), they may report feeling better simply because of their belief — this is called the **placebo effect**. Double and triple blinding removes this source of bias from both sides.
:::

---

## ANOVA — Introduction and Preview

**ANOVA** stands for **Analysis of Variance**. It is an extension of the two-sample t-test to compare **two or more means simultaneously**.

*Professor's note:* "ANOVA is just an extension of the t-test. With the t-test, you're comparing one sample mean or the difference between two means. ANOVA is for more than two means."

| Type | Independent Variables | Dependent Variable | When to use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **One-way ANOVA** | 1 factor | 1 outcome | Comparing 3+ group means on one variable |
| **Two-way ANOVA** | 2 factors | 1 outcome | Testing main effects *and* interaction between two factors |

### ANOVA Hypotheses (one-way, 3 groups)

$$H_0: \mu_1 = \mu_2 = \mu_3 \quad \text{(all means are equal)}$$
$$H_A: \text{at least one mean is different from the others}$$

::: {.callout-warning}
## ⚠️ Important — ANOVA Tells You "If", Not "Where"

*Professor's note:* "ANOVA tells you if there's a difference, or there's not a difference. It doesn't tell you where the differences lie."

After ANOVA, you need a **post-hoc analysis** (e.g., Tukey's test) to identify *which specific pairs* of group means are significantly different.

Example: ANOVA says groups differ → Post-hoc says Group 1 vs Group 2 is significant; Group 1 vs Group 3 is not significant.
:::

### How ANOVA Partitions Variation

ANOVA divides the total variation in the data into two parts:

$$SS_{\text{Total}} = SS_{\text{Between groups}} + SS_{\text{Within groups}}$$

| Source of variation | Also called | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Between groups** | Explained variation | Variation due to the treatment / factor |
| **Within groups** | Unexplained variation / Error | Natural variation within each group |

---

## Effect Size, Sample Size, and Statistical Power

These three quantities are deeply connected. Changing one affects the others. This is the foundation of **power analysis**.

### Effect Size (Cohen's *d*)

**Effect size** measures the *practical significance* of a difference — how large the difference between two means is, relative to the natural variability in the data.

$$d = \frac{\mu_A - \mu_0}{\sigma}$$

Where:

- $\mu_A$ = mean under the **alternative hypothesis** (the "true" mean)
- $\mu_0$ = mean under the **null hypothesis**
- $\sigma$ = population standard deviation

A **larger effect size** = easier to detect the difference = need fewer observations.

::: {.callout-note}
## 💡 Analogy — Effect Size

Imagine trying to hear a whisper versus a shout in a noisy room. The "effect" is the sound level of what you're trying to hear. If the room is very noisy (high variability), you need the sound to be much louder (large effect size) before you can reliably hear it. With a small effect and a lot of noise, you need to listen for a very long time (large sample size) to be sure.
:::

### Statistical Power (1 − β)

**Power** is the probability of correctly detecting a real effect — i.e., correctly rejecting H₀ when it is actually false.

$$\text{Power} = 1 - \beta = P(\text{Reject } H_0 \mid H_0 \text{ is false})$$

- **Low power** → high risk of a **Type II error** (missing a real effect)
- Typical target power is **80%** (0.80) or **90%** (0.90)

*Professor's note:* "The power of the test — given that we wanted to observe an effect of 5 — is an 85% chance of obtaining a significant result when the effect is real."

### The Relationship Between the Four Quantities

For a power analysis, you need **three** of the following four quantities to calculate the **fourth**:

| Quantity | Symbol | Relationship to power |
|---|---|---|
| Sample size | n | ↑ n → ↑ Power |
| Effect size | d | ↑ d → ↑ Power |
| Significance level | α | ↓ α → ↓ Power |
| Power | 1 − β | The target; often set to 0.80 or 0.90 |

Also:

- ↓ Variability (σ) → ↑ Power
- One-tailed test → Higher power than a two-tailed test (for the same n, d, α)

::: {.callout-important}
## ⭐ One-tailed vs. Two-tailed Power

*Professor's note:* "For a given effect size, sample size, and alpha — a one-tailed test is more powerful than a two-tailed test."

In the height exercise: one-tailed power = **85%**, two-tailed power = **76%** (same n, d, and α). The one-tailed test is more powerful because all of the rejection region is on one side.

**When to use a one-tailed test:** Only when you have a specific, pre-stated prediction about the *direction* of the difference AND you have no interest in the opposite outcome.
:::

---

# Key Formulas

### Cohen's d (Effect Size for t-tests)

$$d = \frac{\mu_A - \mu_0}{\sigma}$$

### Statistical Power

$$\text{Power} = 1 - \beta$$

### Standard Error of the Mean

$$SE = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

### General Linear Model

$$Y = \alpha + \beta_1 X_1 + \beta_2 X_2 + \varepsilon$$

### ANOVA Decomposition

$$SS_{\text{Total}} = SS_{\text{Between}} + SS_{\text{Within}}$$

---

# R Code and Practical Examples

## Simulation Exercise — Effect of Sample Size on p-values

The worksheet uses simulated data to show how **sample size** affects hypothesis test conclusions.

```r
set.seed(1)   # ensures results are reproducible

# Case 1: Means differ by 1 (effect size = 1/4 = 0.25), sd = 4
A_10  <- rnorm(10, 0, 4);  B_10  <- rnorm(10, 1, 4)
A_50  <- rnorm(50, 0, 4);  B_50  <- rnorm(50, 1, 4)
A_100 <- rnorm(100, 0, 4); B_100 <- rnorm(100, 1, 4)

t.test(A_10, B_10)    # usually not significant — too few observations
t.test(A_50, B_50)    # may be borderline
t.test(A_100, B_100)  # usually significant — enough power

# Case 2: Very small effect (0.1), same sd = 4
# Need n = 10,000+ to reliably detect this tiny difference
A_10000 <- rnorm(10000, 0, 4); B_10000 <- rnorm(10000, 0.1, 4)
t.test(A_10000, B_10000)
```

### Four Simulation Cases Compared

| Case | Mean A | Mean B | SD | Effect (d) | Sample needed |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | 1 | 4 | 0.25 (small-medium) | ~100s |
| 2 | 0 | 0.1 | 4 | 0.025 (tiny) | ~100,000s |
| 3 | 0 | 0.1 | 1 | 0.1 (very small) | ~10,000s |
| 4 | 0 | 0.2 | 10 | 0.02 (tiny) | ~1,000,000s |

**Key lesson:** Smaller effect size + larger variability = need far more observations to detect the difference.

---

## Power Analysis in R — `pwr` Package

```r
install.packages("pwr")
library(pwr)
```

### Key `pwr` Functions

| Function | Use case |
|---|---|
| `pwr.t.test` | One sample, two sample, or paired t-test |
| `pwr.anova.test` | Balanced one-way ANOVA |
| `pwr.chisq.test` | Chi-square test |
| `pwr.2p.test` | Two proportions (equal n) |
| `pwr.r.test` | Correlation |
| `pwr.f2.test` | General linear model |

For each function, provide **three** of the four: `n`, `d`, `sig.level`, `power` — and the function calculates the fourth (set the fourth to `NULL`).

---

### Worked Example: Chemical Company

A government inspector tests whether a product contains **more than** 4.0 ml of caustic material (the legal limit). She has n = 30 samples, σ = 1.3, and the true mean may be as high as 4.35.

**Step 1: Calculate effect size**

$$d = \frac{4.35 - 4.0}{1.3} = 0.2692$$

**Step 2: Calculate power**

```r
pwr.t.test(n = 30, d = 0.2692, sig.level = 0.05,
           type = "one.sample", alternative = "greater")
# Power = 0.419
```

**Result:** Power ≈ **42%** — only a 42% chance of detecting the violation. The sample size is too small for this effect size.

---

### Exercise 1: Height of Students

$$H_0: \mu = 160 \text{ cm}, \quad H_A: \mu > 160 \text{ cm}$$

Sample: n = 25, $\bar{x}$ = 173.4 cm, s = 9

**Type I Error:** Reject H₀ and conclude mean height > 160 cm *when it is not.*

**Type II Error:** Fail to reject H₀ and conclude mean height ≤ 160 cm *when it actually is greater.*

*Professor's note:* "That question — what are the Type I and Type II errors — is coming up all the time, so it's good to practice it in the context of the question."

**Power (one-tailed, true μ = 165):**

```r
pwr.t.test(n = 25, d = 5/9, sig.level = 0.05,
           type = "one.sample", alternative = "greater")
# Power ≈ 0.85 → 85%
```

**Power (two-tailed, same conditions):**

```r
pwr.t.test(n = 25, d = 5/9, sig.level = 0.05,
           type = "one.sample")
# Power ≈ 0.76 → 76%
```

**Conclusion:** The one-tailed test gives 85% power; the two-tailed gives only 76%. *One-tailed tests are more powerful when you have a clear directional hypothesis.*

---

### Exercise 2: Light Bulb Lifespan

A manufacturer claims bulbs last 850 hours (σ = 50). A consumer group believes the true mean is 810 hours.

$$H_0: \mu = 850, \quad H_A: \mu < 850$$

**Required sample size for 90% power:**

```r
pwr.t.test(d = (810 - 850) / 50, power = 0.9,
           sig.level = 0.05, type = "one.sample",
           alternative = "less")
# n = 14.something → round UP to n = 15
```

**Simulation to verify (10,000 t-tests):**

```r
pvalues <- rep(0, 10000)
for (i in 1:10000) {
  sA <- rnorm(15, 810, 50)
  pvalues[i] <- t.test(sA, y = NULL, "less", 850)$p.value
}
sum(pvalues < 0.05)   # Should be approximately 9,000 (≈ 90%)
```

*Professor's note:* "Just to summarise — what we try to do here in the practical is to show the effect of sample size on the significance of the test. How, if the effect size is small, but the variation is greater, how does that affect what kind of sample size you need?"

---

# Exercise Sheet Mapping

| Exercise / Scenario | Relevant Concept | Statistical Method |
|---|---|---|
| Sugar and tooth decay | Causal association; extraneous variables | Descriptive; identify variable type |
| Coffee and heart disease | Confounding variable (smoking) | Observational study analysis |
| HDL = α + β₁Alcohol + β₂Age | Confounding; observational vs experimental | Linear regression (holds confounders constant) |
| Drug + Vitamin C study | Factors, levels, treatments, factorial design | Two-way ANOVA |
| Osteoporosis drug (50 men/50 women) | Failure of experimental control; bias | Randomised block design fix |
| Chemical company (n=30, μ=4.35) | Underpowered study; one-sample test | `pwr.t.test()` |
| Height study (μ=165, n=25) | Type I/II error; one- vs two-tailed power | `pwr.t.test()` |
| Light bulb (μ=810, power=0.9) | Required sample size calculation | `pwr.t.test()` |

---

# Summary Points

- ✅ **Experimental design** ensures data is collected efficiently. The three core principles are **randomisation**, **blocking**, and **replication**.
- ✅ The key difference between **experimental** and **observational** studies is whether the researcher *manipulates* the conditions. Only experimental studies permit strong causal conclusions.
- ✅ **Association ≠ causation.** Correlation only tells you that variables move together — not that one causes the other.
- ✅ **Extraneous variables** are any variables not of direct interest. When uncontrolled, they can become **confounding variables**.
- ✅ All confounding variables are extraneous — but **not all extraneous variables are confounding**.
- ✅ **Confounding variables** influence *both* the independent and the dependent variable, making it impossible to isolate the true cause.
- ✅ **Spurious correlations** appear real but are coincidences, often driven by a hidden third variable.
- ✅ **Randomised Controlled Trials (RCTs)** are the gold standard for eliminating confounding in experimental research.
- ✅ **ANOVA** compares two or more means; a **post-hoc test** is needed to identify *which* groups differ.
- ✅ **Effect size (d)** = standardised difference between means. Larger effect = easier to detect.
- ✅ **Power** = probability of detecting a real effect. Increases with ↑ n, ↑ d, ↓ σ, one-tailed tests.
- ✅ You need **3 of 4** quantities (n, d, α, power) to calculate the fourth using `pwr.t.test()`.
- ✅ **Type I error (α):** Rejecting H₀ when it is true. Set in advance (typically 0.05).
- ✅ **Type II error (β):** Failing to reject H₀ when it is false. Power = 1 − β.

---

# Pre-Topic Checklist

Before moving to Week 5 (One-Way ANOVA), make sure you can:

- [ ] Explain the difference between an experimental study and an observational study, and give an example of each
- [ ] Name and explain the three principles of experimental design (randomisation, blocking, replication)
- [ ] Distinguish between extraneous, confounding, and spurious variables
- [ ] Identify a confounding variable in a given scenario (e.g., coffee-heart disease-smoking)
- [ ] Define the terms: experimental unit, factor, level, treatment, response variable, explanatory variable
- [ ] Given a study scenario, identify the factors, levels, number of treatments, and appropriate statistical test
- [ ] Explain the difference between single, double, and triple blind designs
- [ ] Explain why randomised controlled trials are considered the gold standard
- [ ] State the null and alternative hypotheses for a one-way ANOVA
- [ ] Explain what ANOVA tells you — and what additional step (post-hoc) is required
- [ ] Calculate Cohen's d (effect size) given two means and a standard deviation
- [ ] Explain how power, sample size, effect size, and α are related
- [ ] Use `pwr.t.test()` in R to calculate power or required sample size
- [ ] Interpret a power value in the context of a question (e.g., "85% chance of detecting a real effect")
- [ ] Describe Type I and Type II errors in the context of a specific research question
- [ ] Explain why a one-tailed test is more powerful than a two-tailed test

---

# Additional Resources

- `pwr` package documentation: <https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/pwr/pwr.pdf>
- G*Power (free software for power analysis): <https://www.psychologie.hhu.de/arbeitsgruppen/allgemeine-psychologie-und-arbeitspsychologie/gpower>
- Spurious Correlations (fun website): <https://www.tylervigen.com/spurious-correlations>

---

# Annotation Space

::: {.callout-note}
## 📝 Personal Annotations

*Use this space to add your own notes, examples, or questions as you review this material.*

\vspace{5cm}
:::

---

*Study guide generated from: class lecture transcript (Paula Harrison), Experimental Design Week 4 lecture slides, and WS2_sample_size_script_STAT9004.R — STAT9004.*
